faq.question: What time is it in Baghdad right now?
faq.answer: Baghdad operates on Arabia Standard Time (AST), UTC+3 year-round. The city maintains a consistent +3 hour offset from Coordinated Universal Time throughout the year, with no daylight saving time adjustments since 2007.

faq.question: What time zone is Baghdad in?
faq.answer: Baghdad uses Arabia Standard Time (AST), UTC+3. Iraq permanently adopted this timezone in 2007 and does not observe daylight saving time, making it one of the few Middle Eastern capitals with year-round stable time.

faq.question: Does Baghdad observe daylight saving time?
faq.answer: No. Iraq abolished daylight saving time in 2007 and has maintained Arabia Standard Time (UTC+3) year-round since then, providing consistent scheduling for business and international coordination.

faq.question: What is the time difference between Baghdad and New York?
faq.answer: Baghdad is 8 hours ahead of New York during Eastern Standard Time (November-March) and 7 hours ahead during Eastern Daylight Time (March-November). When it's 9 AM in New York, it's 5 PM in Baghdad during EST or 4 PM during EDT.

faq.question: What is the time difference between Baghdad and London?
faq.answer: Baghdad is 3 hours ahead of London during Greenwich Mean Time (October-March) and 2 hours ahead during British Summer Time (March-October). When it's noon in London, it's 3 PM in Baghdad during GMT or 2 PM during BST.

faq.question: What time does the stock exchange open in Baghdad?
faq.answer: The Iraq Stock Exchange (ISX) operates Sunday through Thursday from 10:00 AM to 12:30 PM AST (UTC+3), with trading sessions lasting 2.5 hours. The exchange is closed on Fridays and Saturdays, following the Middle Eastern weekend schedule.

faq.question: What is the best time to call Baghdad from the US?
faq.answer: Call Baghdad between 11 PM and 7 AM US Eastern Time to reach business hours (7 AM to 3 PM in Baghdad). From Pacific Time, call between 8 PM and 4 AM. Early morning US calls align with Baghdad's afternoon business window.

faq.question: What are typical business hours in Baghdad?
faq.answer: Standard business hours in Baghdad run Sunday through Thursday from 8:00 AM to 3:00 PM AST, with many government offices closing by 2:00 PM. The Iraqi workweek follows the Middle Eastern pattern with Friday-Saturday weekends. Banking hours typically extend until 4:00 PM.

seo_description: Current time in Baghdad, Iraq — Arabia Standard Time (AST), UTC+3. Live clock, timezone converter, and time difference calculator for Baghdad.
seo_title: Current Time in Baghdad — Timezone, UTC+3 & World Clock

content_blocks.title: Baghdad Time Zone and UTC Offset Explained
content_blocks.content: Baghdad operates on Arabia Standard Time (AST) with a UTC+3 offset throughout the entire year. Since Iraq abolished daylight saving time in 2007, Baghdad maintains consistent time year-round, eliminating the seasonal clock changes that affect many global cities. This stability makes Baghdad aligned with other permanent UTC+3 zones including Kuwait, Saudi Arabia (Riyadh), and Qatar. The decision to stop observing DST was made to reduce confusion in government operations and improve coordination with neighboring Gulf states. Baghdad shares the same timezone as East Africa nations like Kenya and Ethiopia, despite being geographically in Western Asia. For international business planning, Baghdad's fixed offset means meeting times remain constant regardless of season, unlike cities such as New York or London where DST shifts create varying time differences. The IANA timezone database identifies Baghdad as Asia/Baghdad, and this timezone covers all of Iraq from Basra in the south to Mosul in the north.

content_blocks.title: Business Hours and Working Schedule in Baghdad
content_blocks.content: Baghdad follows a Sunday-to-Thursday work week, reflecting Middle Eastern business culture where Friday and Saturday constitute the weekend. Government offices typically operate from 8:00 AM to 2:00 PM AST, while private sector businesses extend hours from 8:00 AM to 3:00 PM or 4:00 PM. Banks in Baghdad generally serve customers until 4:00 PM on working days. This schedule creates specific overlap windows for international coordination: Baghdad's morning (8 AM-12 PM AST) coincides with early morning in Europe (5 AM-9 AM GMT) and late night in the United States (12 AM-4 AM EST). For optimal business communication with Western partners, Baghdad professionals often schedule calls between 2:00 PM and 5:00 PM local time, when European offices are fully operational and US East Coast teams are starting their day. Use our meeting planner to find ideal times that work across Baghdad's UTC+3 timezone and your location. The Iraq Stock Exchange operates abbreviated hours from 10:00 AM to 12:30 PM Sunday through Thursday, with significantly shorter trading windows than Western exchanges. Understanding these timing constraints is crucial for international investors and businesses coordinating with Iraqi partners.

content_blocks.title: International Calling Times for Baghdad Coordination
content_blocks.content: Coordinating calls with Baghdad requires careful timing due to significant time differences with Western business centers. From the United States Eastern Time zone, Baghdad is 8 hours ahead in winter (EST) and 7 hours ahead in summer (EDT) when America observes daylight saving. This means calling Baghdad at 9:00 AM EST reaches the city at 5:00 PM AST, near the end of the business day. The optimal window for US-based callers is between 12:00 AM and 7:00 AM Eastern Time to reach Baghdad's core business hours of 8:00 AM to 3:00 PM. West Coast callers on Pacific Time should dial between 9:00 PM and 4:00 AM to connect during Baghdad working hours. European coordination is more convenient: London to Baghdad involves just 3 hours difference in winter and 2 hours in summer, meaning a London 9:00 AM call reaches Baghdad at noon or 11:00 AM respectively. For Asia-Pacific coordination, Tokyo is 6 hours ahead of Baghdad, while Dubai shares the same UTC+3 timezone, enabling seamless real-time communication. Business travelers should note that Baghdad's lack of DST means time differences with DST-observing countries shift twice yearly, requiring calendar adjustments for recurring international meetings scheduled months in advance.
